name: Severance Hill
short_description: A modest rockhounding site in central New Hampshire's White Mountain foothills. Hunt for quartz, feldspar, and mica in outcrops and weathered granite exposures along this relatively flat hilltop.
description:
Severance Hill sits within the White Mountain region of New Hampshire, an area shaped by Precambrian metamorphic rocks and younger granite intrusions. The 1508-foot elevation and gentle slopes make this an accessible destination for collectors seeking common but beautiful New England minerals. The site offers exposure to pegmatitic and granitic formations typical of this part of the Granite State.

The geology here reflects the Acadian orogeny, a major mountain-building event that deformed and metamorphosed rocks roughly 400 million years ago. Subsequent granite emplacement during the Devonian period created the mineral-rich formations found today. Weathering and glacial activity have exposed these rocks, making surface collecting feasible year-round.

Rockhounders visiting Severance Hill should expect to find quartz in various forms, including clear to milky specimens, along with feldspar (often orthoclase) and biotite mica. Beryl occasionally appears in pegmatitic pockets. The flat terrain and moderate elevation make this a good beginner-friendly introduction to New England rockhounding geology.

Access and specific collecting areas may vary by season and land management status. Always confirm current conditions and regulations before visiting. The site is best explored during dry months when footing is secure and visibility is good.
history: Severance Hill lies within the White Mountain granitic belt, formed during the Devonian period when magma intruded into Precambrian metamorphic country rock. This intrusion created pegmatitic zones rich in feldspar, quartz, and mica that persist today. The area saw modest historical quarrying activity typical of central New Hampshire, where granite and feldspar have been extracted for industrial use since the 19th century.
tips: Examine exposed granite outcrops and weathered ledges on the hilltop slopes for quartz seams and feldspar crystals. Bring a 10x hand lens to identify mica species and check for beryl in pegmatitic pockets. Focus your search along fracture zones where mineral-rich fluids once flowed, and collect samples from loose material around weathered boulders rather than attempting to break solid rock.
terrain_notes: Elevation 1508 feet with flat to gently rolling terrain. Mixed deciduous and coniferous forest cover typical of central New Hampshire foothills. Granite outcrops and boulders scattered across the hilltop. Footing is generally stable on dry ground but can be slippery when wet. Walking distance from parking area likely 0.5 to 1 mile depending on access point.
best_season: May - October. Late spring through early fall offers dry ground, secure footing, and clear visibility of rock exposures. Winter snow cover and ice make the site hazardous; mud and wet conditions in early spring reduce accessibility.
written_directions: From Danbury town center, take NH Route 104 east approximately 1.5 miles. Look for Severance Hill road or local access signs on the north side of the highway. A small pullout or parking area may exist near the base of the hill. Confirm public access status locally before proceeding, as land ownership may affect access rights.
road_conditions: Paved highway access via NH 104. Local access roads may be unpaved; passenger vehicles adequate for main routes.
parking_notes: Small informal pullout likely near the base of Severance Hill along NH 104 or local access road. Limited capacity. Confirm current access and parking availability with local authorities.
collection_rules: Rockhounding on public lands in New Hampshire is generally permitted for personal use and hobby collection. Remove only loose material and surface samples. Do not damage outcrops or attempt motorized excavation. Respect any posted signs indicating private property or restricted areas. Collection of rare minerals or gems may be regulated; stick to common quartz, feldspar, and mica varieties.
rules:
Collect loose material and surface samples only
No motorized excavation or hammering of exposed bedrock
No commercial collection
Respect private property boundaries
Leave the site in good condition
nearest_services: Danbury, approximately 2 miles south. Limited services; nearest full-service town is Laconia, approximately 15 miles south.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat hilltop terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Uneven rocky ground and scattered boulders require sturdy footwear. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Walking distance from parking is short to moderate. Suitable for children and older adults in good mobility condition.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at Severance Hill in New Hampshire?
faq.answer: Severance Hill is known for quartz (clear, milky, and smoky varieties), feldspar (primarily orthoclase), and biotite mica. Collectors also occasionally find beryl in pegmatitic pockets within the granite exposures. These are all common New England minerals typical of the White Mountain granite belt.

faq.question: Is Severance Hill a free rockhounding site, or do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Severance Hill appears to be open for casual public rockhounding without a permit or fee. However, always verify current access status with local Danbury authorities or the landowner, as access policies may change. Respect all posted signs and private property boundaries.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound at Severance Hill near Danbury?
faq.answer: Visit from May through October for optimal conditions. Dry ground and clear visibility make it easier to spot mineral specimens on exposed outcrops. Winter snow and ice create hazardous footing, while spring mud can obscure rocks and make the site slippery.

faq.question: Is Severance Hill beginner-friendly for rockhounders new to New Hampshire?
faq.answer: Yes, Severance Hill is excellent for beginners. The flat terrain, short hikes, and easy-to-identify minerals like quartz and mica make it accessible to families and amateur collectors. The site requires no special equipment or expertise, making it ideal for learning basic field geology.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Severance Hill?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a 10x hand lens for identifying feldspar and mica varieties, a small collection bag, and a notebook. A basic geology field guide for New England minerals is helpful. No hammers or picks are needed if you stick to loose material and weathered samples.

faq.question: How far is Severance Hill from Danbury, and is cell service available?
faq.answer: Severance Hill is approximately 2 miles northeast of Danbury town center via NH Route 104. Cell service is generally reliable in this area since it is within the central New Hampshire foothills with good network coverage, though coverage may be spotty high on the hill itself.

meta_description: Hunt quartz, feldspar, and mica at Severance Hill near Danbury, NH. Free public access, beginner-friendly terrain, easy hiking. Explore White Mountain granite geology.
